title: Modern Humans and Neanderthals May Have Shared a Cave-Dwelling Culture
gist: A PNAS study of Üçağızlı II Cave in southern Türkiye finds Neanderthals occupied the site 77,000-59,000 years ago and Homo sapiens took over until 47,000 years ago, suggesting the two groups shared not just genes but a cave-dwelling culture.

insights.claim: A PNAS study of Üçağızlı II Cave on Türkiye's Mediterranean coast dates Neanderthal occupation to 77,000-59,000 years ago, followed by Homo sapiens who lived there until 47,000 years ago.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Concrete site-level timeline sharpens models of when and where the two lineages coexisted.

insights.claim: The Levant functioned as a major migration corridor ~60,000 years ago, with humans leaving Africa and Neanderthals moving south from Europe overlapping and likely interbreeding.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Frames the geography of early human dispersal and admixture.

insights.claim: Modern humans and Neanderthals are known to have shared genes during their overlap, and the new evidence extends that overlap to shared material culture at a single site.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Shared culture, not just genes, reframes the two as interacting societies.
